content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 12, 199 o
Luis A. Rodriguez
senior Assistant city Attorney
city of orange
300 E. Chapnan Avenue
orange, CA 92666
Re3 Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. r-90-272 Revised
Dear Mr. Rodriguez:
You are seeking advice on behaLf of City of Orange Mayor Don
E. smith, regarding his duties and responsibi I ities under the
conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
n6s1't1.1 Pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy encfosed), we provide
you with advice as Mr. Smith's authorj.zed representative, . but as
your request for advice does not refer to a specific pending gov-
ernmentil decision, we are treating your question-as a request for
informal assistance on1y. (Regulation 18329 (b) . ) a
The follor^/ing advice is based upon the facts provided in your
l-etters of April 2 and April L7 , L99o, and in your telephone
conversations with this agency on March 28, April 16, and
April 23, 1990.
OUESTION
A decision pending before the city concerns the acquisition
of property located within a redevelopment project area, for sub-
."qi.,".,i deielopment. litayor Srnith owns numerous rental properties
wi€trin 2,500 feet of the property that is the subject of the
acquisition decision. Can he participate in this decision?
1 co\r"r.rrn"nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. AIl statutory references
are to the Governnent Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Conmission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations . Section
18500, et seq-.- AlI references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vision e of tne California code of Regul-ations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice' 
(sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4'O8O7 . (916)322'5660


File No. T-9o-272
Page 2
CONCLUSION
Mayor snith nay participate in the decision to acquire the
property provided the decision wiII not have a foreseeable rnate-
rial financial inpact on any of the properties he owns in the area
of the property the subject of which the decision is pending.
FACTS
The orange Redevelopment Agency (trthe Agencyrr) owns 1.9 acres
in the Southwest Redevelopment Project Area and is considering
acquiring an additional 1.56 acres in the project area. This ad-
ditional acreage (ttthe acquisition sitett) and six privately olrned
adjacent acres were the subject of a prelininary development fea-
sibility 
study in 1988; the study was undertaken by the Agency to
ascertain developrnent of the site and the surrounding area for
public and private use.
There have been no decisions concerning the acquisition site
made by the Agency, either for its acquisition or for its appro-
priate use. Ho$rever, the Agency has established a subcommittee,
Lhaired by Mayor Smith, to 'rconduct negotiationsri concernj,ng site
acquisition and use.
Mayor Srnith o$/ns a variety of residential rental properties
within 2,500 feet of the t$/o acre site.r 
You have provided an
analysis from a professional real estate appraiser which concludes
that the city's announcenent of its intention to purchase the
acquisition site would not inpact any of the individual properties
by either $1o,ooo in market value or $1,000 in rental value per
12-month period.
The Letter fron the appraisers only contains their judgement
concerning the financial inpact of the city's decision to announce
its intention to purchase the 1.59 acres. No appraisal is
provided with respect to the inpact on any of the llayor's proper-
ties as a result of any decision concerning the development or use
of the acres once acquired.
3 Yo,, have indicated the approximate distances from the two acre
site as foLl-ows3 the E. sycamore single family residences and the
N. citrus duplexes, L,oo0 feet; the N. Batavia duPlexes and
w. Palnyra duplex, 1,500 feet; the N. Grand apartments and the
E. walnut single fanily residence, 1,70o feeti the S. clark
duplexes and triplexes, 2,Ooo feet; and the E. chapman office
buj.lding, and the single family homes on E. Sycamore, E. Maple,
and N. cleveland, 2,500 feet.


Fife No. I-9o-2'72
Page 3
ANALYSIS
The Act prohj.bits a public official 
from making, participat-
j,ng in, or using his official 
position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to kno$, he has a finan-
cial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A public official 
has a finan-
cial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeabLe that
the decision will have a :naterial financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally, on the official, 
a
member of his inmediate farnily, or on any real property in which
the official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
($1,ooo) or more. (Section 87103(b).)
Because he is the mayor of the city of orange, an elected
rnember of the city council, and a member of the city's redevelop-
ment agency, Mr. Snith is a public official. 
(Section 82048.)
Theref5re,-Mr. Smith must disqualify hinself from any city council
or redevelopment agency decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financiat effect on hirn or on his real
property interest that is distinguishable from the public gener-
aIIy. 
(Section 87103 (b) . )
Foroseeable uaterial FiDalrcial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it rrif I occur. while certainty
is not required, an effect that is merely a possibility- is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downev communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (],975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198. )
We have previously advised that the anticipated result of a
redeveloprnent plan, whose purpose is the promotion of sound de-
velopmenl and Lhe irnprovement of blighted areas, is an increase in
property vafues and an inproved business climate ( Brol^,n Advice
Letter i-eg-slz; In re olqesbv (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71 (copies en-
closed. ) Thus it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions
regarding the redeveloprnent plan witl have a financial effect on
property located in and near the redevelopment area.
In order to determine l^rhether the foreseeable effect of a
decision is material. as to real property in which an official 
has
an interest, the Cornrnission has adopted Regulation 1A7O2.3 (copy
enclosed), You have inforned us that each of the eleven rental
properties owned by Mr. snith is located from 1,000 to 2,500 feet
from the acquisition site. 
For projects between 300 and 2,50o
feet fron an official's 
property, the effect of the decision will
be considered naterial if it witt have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official 
has an interestl or


File No. l-9o-272
Page 4
(B) will affect the rental value of the prop-
erty by S1,ooo ore more per 12 month period.
(Resulation ta702.3 (a) (3) (A) and (B) . )
whether the effect of the decision is positive or negative i-s of
no consequence under the Act. 
(Younq Advice Letter, A-89-149,
copy enclosed. )
A professional aPpraiser has determined that the impact on
Mayor Smith's properties resultino frqm.the anlouncgment of the
city's intention to purchase the acquisition site will not meet
the requisite threshold arnounts as provided in Regulation
La7o2. i(a) (s).f 
However, Section 87103 focuses on the effect of
the decision to purchase the acquisition property. If the irnpact
of such a purcha;e decision on each of the eleven properties you
have indiv-idualIy identified wilt falt below the financial
threshol-ds speciiied in the regulation, his partj.cipation in the
decision to lurchase the acquisition property would be perlTritted '
This advice j.s lirnited to the pending decision before the
Agency with respect to the purchase of the acquisition site. 
You
trive indicated that l'/hen purchased, the site -- along with the 1'9
acres already owned by the Agency, will be subject to a series of
decisions concerning an appropriate use for the proPerty. Because
Mayor Snith's property is located.within 2,500 feet from the
pr6perty, the lamL anllysis apptying the thresholds of Regulation
iatoz.: f il fsl $rould be iequired to deterrnine whether he was able
to particiiaie 
in the use decisions, and if any of the eleven
proierties^were impacted by anounts at or above the requisite
tnrLstrota, his participation in the use decision would be
prohibited.5 tire apprliser's opinion that you have provided is
-applicable only to the announcenent by and the decj-sion of the
city to purchase the acquisition site.
4 Th" cornmission does not act as a finder of fact. 
The advice
provided in this letter is applicable only to the extent the facts
provided are correct and that all naterial facts have been pre-
sented. (In re Oqlesbv, suPra.)
5 Reqlulation 18702.3(d) provides sone factors to consider in
deterrnining the rnagnitude of the financial irnpact on the nayor's
property. such faators include the proxinity of the his proper-
iiei 
ana the nagnitude of the proposed project in relationship to
his 
properties, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision on the redevelopnent Property witl affect the developluent
potential or income producing potential of the nayor's properties,
ind whether it is relsonably foreseeable that the acquisition pro-
perty decision wiII result in a change to the character of the
neigtrforhood including, but not lirnited to, the effect on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air ernissions or
sirniLar traits.


File
Page
SH:」SR:plh
Enc10Sures
I-90-272
No.
5
I trust this Letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Acting
counsel, Legal Division
